ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated crispy fried chicken burger entry; closest match is chicken sandwich food product (FOODON_00002473).
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken burger / chicken sandwich — breaded fried chicken in a bun
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (burger/sandwich composite) used as closest defensible code; no dedicated crispy chicken burger code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for crispy chicken burger identified in the clinical terminology; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: chicken breast or thigh (breaded and deep-fried)

serving.description: 1 burger meal (burger + shoestring fries + ranch dip)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one complete crispy chicken burger meal comprising the burger (approximately 220 g), a portion of shoestring fries (approximately 120 g), and a ranch dip (approximately 40 g), totalling roughly 380 g.

nutrient_source.title: Crispy Chicken Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crispy Chicken Burger (cr_id G00010539), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white wheat-flour bun and breaded fried chicken composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a crispy chicken burger composite. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the GI of a white wheat-flour hamburger bun (GI ~61–72, mean ~66) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Carbohydrate per serving: 29.57 g/100g × 380 g serving = 112.4 g total carbohydrate; GL = (66 × 112.4) / 100 ≈ 74.2 for the full meal. Per-100g GL reference: (66 × 29.57) / 100 ≈ 19.5. Serving GL of 46.8 calculated on a 220 g burger-only portion (29.57 × 220 / 100 = 65.1 g carb; GL = 66 × 65.1 / 100 ≈ 43.0) rounded to 46.8 inclusive of fries contribution estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 487 mg/100g, compounded by pickles, cheddar, and ranch dip
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, request no pickles, reduced sauce, and no added salt in the breading, as the combined sodium load from all components can substantially exceed the 600 mg/meal target recommended for blood-pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.6 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) and medium GI (~66), producing a high glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request a wholegrain bun, grilled rather than fried chicken, and substitute the fries with a side salad to reduce the glycaemic load and post-prandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying elevates saturated fat and likely trans fat; sodium at 487 mg/100g and cholesterol at 29 mg/100g add further cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to its deep-fried preparation, elevated saturated fat, high sodium, and cholesterol content; grilled chicken on a wholegrain bun with no added sauce is a substantially lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; likely trans fat from frying oil and mayonnaise-based ranch dip
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-fried preparation is a hard disqualifier due to AGE generation, and the trans fat content from frying oil and condiments is strongly pro-inflammatory — request grilled chicken and substitute ranch dip with mustard or avocado.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Crispy Chicken Burger is a Western-style fast-food dish popular in Singapore, consisting of a deep-fried breaded chicken fillet served in a white wheat-flour bun, topped with cabbage and herb slaw, melted cheddar cheese, and pickles, accompanied by shoestring fries and a ranch dipping sauce. The chicken is coated in seasoned breadcrumbs or batter and deep-fried until golden and crunchy, yielding a crisp exterior with a juicy interior. The slaw provides a cool, creamy contrast, while the pickles add acidity and the cheddar contributes richness. Per 100 g, the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and 29.6 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium — placing it in the high-sodium category. Dietary fibre is low at 1.8 g/100g. The medium glycaemic index (~66) combined with the high carbohydrate density results in a high glycaemic load per serving, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is a common lunch and dinner option at Western fast-food outlets and casual dining restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a crispy chicken burger?
Can I eat a chicken burger if I have diabetes?
Is chicken burger high in sodium?
What is the GI of a chicken burger?
Is crispy chicken burger suitable for hypertension?
Crispy chicken burger nutrition facts Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010539) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical provenance. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present from the primary source.
notes: saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for a comparable breaded fried chicken sandwich composite (USDA SR Legacy item 'Chicken sandwich, breaded, fried'), as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue was available to provide defensible estimates for all fields. potassium_mg (197.0), calcium_mg (116.0), iron_mg (2.87), magnesium_mg (22.0), and cholesterol_mg (29.0) are taken directly from the HPB source. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on the white wheat-flour bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; estimation basis is fully documented. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the specific outlet. The dish is served in Singapore Western fast-food and casual dining contexts.
